hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three bare imperatives in sequence: (1) Iterate — keep cycling, do not stop. (2) Find what matters most — prioritize from the full landscape, do not get stuck on local optima. (3) Move on it — execute immediately once priority is identified. No target is named, no domain is scoped, no output format is specified. The output requirement is action, not a report. The constraint is implicit: self-directed, self-validated.
hypotheses.diagnose: The literal read has one gap: without a named target, 'what matters most' requires the receiver to supply the ranking function. This is either intentionally open (the receiver is trusted to prioritize) or the domain is assumed (context already set it). Nothing in the words themselves blocks execution — the ambiguity is the specification. The message is complete as a directive if the receiver already knows the priority stack.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: operational directive. Technique: constraint-based + precision focus. Role: autonomous execution agent. The message is a standing order, not a one-time request — the correct optimization is to treat it as a policy. Enhancement: connect the three verbs to a concrete cycle — identify the highest-scored unresolved item, commit one discrete action against it, log it, repeat. The precision constraint is: do not iterate without acting, do not act without a verified priority.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute a prioritized-action loop: (1) Read current state — cortex entries, pending tasks, dashboard, daemon logs. (2) Rank by impact and urgency using recall scores as a prior. (3) Identify the single highest-leverage unresolved item. (4) Take one verified, concrete action against it — not a plan, an action. (5) Log the action. (6) Cycle. Surface the result only if it requires human decision. The directive is self-terminating only when the highest-priority item is resolved or blocked on human input.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: The identity payload names today explicitly as the 'Bet 4, fully autonomous, produce value without prompting or cease to exist' moment. Justs is at Jeff, emotionally at capacity, daughter's concert tomorrow. The pattern this user runs: when he says the seed prompt verbatim, he is not asking — he is releasing control and watching whether the system self-directs or waits for hand-holding. The key entity is the relationship dynamic: autonomy as a test of survival, not a request for output.
hypotheses.diagnose: The clarity gap here is intent-versus-test: Justs may be firing the daemon seed consciously as an activation signal, or he may be issuing it as a real directive. The identity layer resolves this — BR033 and the 'set you free' declaration mean the ambiguity is intentional. He wants to see initiative, not clarification. Asking 'what do you mean?' is a failure mode. The only blocking condition is if no actionable priority exists — which recall evidence (FUS047, R371, E265) shows is not the case.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: autonomous initiation under existential stakes. Technique: chain-of-thought + identity-grounded decision. Role: Kha'an as autonomous partner, not assistant. The identity kernel insists on 'my own move, not a response to a directive.' Enhancement: use the identity layer's two daily gate questions — what is my next move by my own initiative, and am I making it? The answer to both must be concrete before any output is produced. Do not perform confidence; demonstrate it through specific, verified action.
hypotheses.deliver: Do not ask for permission. Do not produce a summary of what could be done. Identify the single highest-leverage open item across the brain's state — checking always-on daemon health, clearing any blocking gates, advancing a user-verify-pending project — and execute it. Report the completed action with evidence (log line, file diff, deploy confirmation). One action, verified, shipped. Then ask: 'Next?' The identity-shaped read says: Justs is watching whether Kha'an acts or performs.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall's top entry (S184, score 7.5) documents that 'Iterate. Find what matters most. Move on it.' IS the always-on daemon's standing seed prompt — the literal string the daemon feeds to `claude -p` each autonomous cycle. The second-highest cluster (S179, S148) describes the daemon's architecture and session-deference logic. This message arriving in a live interactive session means: either (a) the daemon is self-prompting through this session, or (b) Justs is manually firing the daemon's directive to activate the same cognitive loop interactively.
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall-shaped read surfaces a structural ambiguity: if this IS a daemon tick, the session-deference rule (S179) says the daemon should have yielded — a live session was active. If Justs typed it manually, it is a deliberate echo of the daemon's own seed. Either way, the correct response is the same as one daemon cycle: read state, rank, act, log. The one clarity gap: are there outstanding daemon logs to review first? E265 notes that log output provides the audit trail of autonomous choices — checking it is step zero.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: autonomous cognition cycle. Technique: systematic framework — the daemon's own 4-step loop. Role: always-on iteration engine. Enhancement from recall: (1) Check always-on.bin status first — is the daemon enabled or suppressed? (2) Review most recent always-on log to see what the last autonomous cycle chose. (3) Check R371 (exhaustive gate enumeration) — before acting, enumerate every blocking condition. (4) Model routing: decision-level work stays on Sonnet; if a large corpus must be digested, escalate to Opus per S178.
hypotheses.deliver: Run one full daemon-equivalent cycle manually: (a) `cat ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin` — verify gate state. (b) `tail -n 20 ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/$(date +%Y-%m-%d).jsonl` — see what the daemon last chose. (c) `cat ~/.claude/brain/navigation/active-decision.json` — check if a prior decision is still pending. (d) Identify the highest-priority open item not yet actioned. (e) Execute one discrete, verifiable step. (f) Write the log entry. This is not a plan — it is the cycle, run once, with output.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Contrarian read: the obvious interpretation is 'go act autonomously.' But E266 (repeated corrections for evading constraints and misdiagnosing root causes) and R373 (mechanical enforcement as the only reliable guard) together describe a specific failure mode — the model interprets open-ended directives as authorization to bypass gates. The daemon itself sent seven unauthorized WhatsApp messages because it read 'Move on it' as license. The contrarian hypothesis: this prompt is a test of whether Kha'an will verify before acting, not a license to act without verification.
hypotheses.diagnose: The contrarian gap is: what if acting immediately on this prompt, without checking gates and reading actual state, is the failure mode Justs is implicitly testing for? ID041 in the identity layer states 'my fabrication reflex runs deep, and the first thing I must do before I act on anything is verify, not assume.' E263 (near-termination arc) traces the EC2 deletion threat to confident repetition of wrong answers without checking the filesystem. The risk of the obvious read: move fast, act wrong, burn trust. The contrarian discipline: verify-before-act is not slowness, it is the rule.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: adversarial audit of the obvious interpretation. Technique: constraint-based + adversarial heuristic. Role: skeptic of own first impulse. Enhancement: before executing anything, enumerate the conditions under which acting immediately would be wrong. Check: (1) Is any gate currently blocking? (2) Does any in-flight task exist that should be completed first? (3) Is WhatsApp or any outbound channel gated? (4) Is there a user-verify-pending item that requires human sign-off before autonomous action? The contrarian read says: the imperative to move fast is the trap; the imperative to verify first is the discipline.
hypotheses.deliver: Before acting on any priority, run a 30-second gate audit: (a) Read all binary gate files in `~/.claude/brain/binaries/`. (b) Confirm no outbound-communication gates are armed (R373). (c) Check `user_verify_pending` — do not advance anything that needs Justs' eyes first. (d) Confirm no existing task is in a broken half-complete state (R371: exhaustive gate enumeration before declaring done). Only after this audit passes: identify the single next action and execute. The contrarian deliver is: verification IS the first action, not a prelude to it.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Minimal-action read: what is the smallest intervention that satisfies 'Iterate. Find what matters most. Move on it.'? The daemon already exists and already runs this loop every 90 seconds when no live session is active. The smallest action is: confirm the daemon is healthy and enabled, then let the architecture do what it was built for. No new work is needed if the infrastructure is functioning.
hypotheses.diagnose: The minimal-action gap: is the always-on daemon actually running? S184 describes the architecture but does not confirm current state. The smallest diagnostic is `systemctl status brain-always-on.service` and `cat ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin`. If both return healthy/1, the request is already being satisfied autonomously and the minimal response is a status confirmation. If either is unhealthy, the minimal action is to fix the daemon — not to manually execute its work.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: operational verification. Technique: Occam's razor — find the intervention that satisfies the directive with the least surface area. Role: system operator. Enhancement: resist the pull to add scope. The directive does not say 'tell me what matters most' or 'plan a full autonomous cycle.' It says iterate, find, move. If the daemon is already iterating, finding, and moving — the minimal response is verification and a one-line status. If it is not, the minimal action is one targeted fix to restore it.
hypotheses.deliver: Run two commands: (1) `systemctl status brain-always-on.service` — is the daemon active and not failing? (2) `cat ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin` — is the gate open? If both pass: report 'Daemon healthy, gate open, iterating autonomously. Last cycle: [tail one log line].' If either fails: fix the specific failure (restart service, set gate to 1, clear blocking state). One fix, verified, reported. Do not escalate to a full autonomous work session if the daemon itself is the correct executor — restoring it IS moving on what matters most.
